meta.title: Cognitive Intelligence Test
meta.description: A self-reflection test inspired by dimensions used in general cognitive ability assessments like the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale (WAIS), exploring how you tend to reason, solve problems, and process information across 10 cognitive dimensions.

scale.minLabel: Not at all true of you

levels.label: Exceptional Reasoner
levels.desc: You consistently show sharp, flexible thinking — spotting patterns, solving problems, and reasoning through complexity with real confidence. This looks like a strong, reliable cognitive strength for you.

levels.label: Sharp & Capable
levels.desc: You reason clearly and solve problems well in most situations, picking up patterns and logic quickly. A few specific areas may still need more deliberate effort.

levels.label: Solid Thinker
levels.desc: You generally reason and solve problems effectively, though unfamiliar or complex situations can sometimes slow you down.

levels.label: Developing Thinker
levels.desc: Your reasoning is inconsistent — strong in some areas, less confident in others. Focused practice in a few key dimensions could help a lot.

levels.label: Emerging Thinker
levels.desc: Problem-solving and reasoning tasks often feel effortful right now. Small, consistent practice could meaningfully build these skills over time.

levels.label: Building Foundations
levels.desc: Your answers suggest these reasoning skills are still developing. This is a good starting point for focused, patient practice.

questions.text: You can quickly understand the main point of a complicated paragraph.
You can explain a complex idea in simple terms so others understand it easily.
You pick up on the underlying meaning of a conversation, not just the words spoken.
You can follow multi-step verbal instructions without needing them repeated.
You notice when a sentence is ambiguous and can work out what it likely means.
You can summarize a long article accurately in just a few sentences.
You understand jokes and wordplay quickly, even when they're subtle.
You can follow the logic of a speaker's argument as they build it point by point.
You catch inconsistencies in what someone is telling you while they're still speaking.
You can rephrase technical language into everyday terms for someone unfamiliar with the topic.
You understand the tone and intent behind a message, not just its literal content.
You can read between the lines of a formal letter or email.
You grasp new concepts explained verbally without needing a visual aid.
You can identify the weakest point in a written argument.
You understand analogies and metaphors as soon as you hear them.
You can hold a complex verbal explanation in mind while someone finishes speaking.
You notice when a speaker contradicts something they said earlier.
You can distinguish fact from opinion in something you've just read or heard.
You understand instructions written in a dense, formal style without much trouble.
You can identify the main argument in a debate even when it's poorly organized.
You often need someone to reword instructions before you understand what to do.
You lose track of the point when a conversation gets complicated.
You find it hard to summarize what you just read.
You struggle to follow arguments that have more than two or three steps.
You often miss the underlying message in what someone says.
You can quickly figure out what a confusing question is actually asking.
You can tell when a written passage is trying to persuade rather than just inform.
You understand unfamiliar vocabulary from its context without needing a dictionary.

You often forget the exact meaning of words you've learned before.

You understand specialized vocabulary from fields you don't work in, like law or medicine.

You avoid using certain words because you're not fully sure what they mean.
You often have to look up words that come up in normal conversation.
You can distinguish between near-synonyms like "tired," "exhausted," and "weary."
You recognize when a word has a technical meaning that differs from its everyday use.
You can quickly name a category that a group of words belongs to.
You understand words with multiple layers of meaning, like puns rely on.
You can identify a more vivid or precise word to replace a bland one.
